summary: DeVault invites developers to build their systems programming projects using a new language (Hare, unnamed at the time) to help drive development of its standard library. He pitches the language as simple, robust, and well-suited to low-level tasks like OS kernels, shells, and networking software. The post is a direct call to action for adventurous early adopters willing to work with an immature ecosystem. He later added a note that enough projects had signed on.
key_points: DeVault is recruiting early adopters to build real projects in the then-unnamed Hare language to stress-test and improve its standard library
The language is pitched as simple, robust, with static typing, manual memory management, and a minimal runtime
Target use cases are classic systems programming: coreutils, kernels, shells, GUI toolkits, databases, web servers
The strategy is community-driven development where real projects drive language and library maturity
money_quotes: If you're feeling adventurous, I would like you to give it a crack in our new systems programming language, and to use it to drive improvements in the less-developed areas of our standard library.
XXXX is a systems programming language designed to be simple and robust.
XXXX uses a static type system, manual memory management, and a minimal runtime.
It is well-suited to writing operating systems, system tools, compilers, networking software, and other low-level, high performance tasks.

Whatever your systems programming use-case, we think that our language is likely to be a good fit for you, and your help in proving that, and spurring development to rise to meet your needs, would be quite welcome.
key_insight: The best way to mature a new programming language is to recruit adventurous developers to build real projects in it, letting practical needs drive standard library development.
